parentQuestion: Tinnitus, vertigo, and gradual hearing loss typify the disorder called ________.

scaffolds.q: Tinnitus is the perception of ringing, buzzing, or roaring in the ears.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Tinnitus is 'phantom' sound — perceived without external stimulus.

scaffolds.q: Vertigo is a sensation of spinning or rotational dizziness.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Vertigo indicates vestibular system dysfunction — not just lightheadedness.

scaffolds.q: Sensorineural hearing loss affects the inner ear or auditory nerve.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Damage to cochlear hair cells or auditory nerve causes this type of hearing loss.

scaffolds.q: The cochlea (hearing) and vestibular system (balance) share the inner ear space.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! A disorder affecting one can often affect the other.

scaffolds.q: Ménière's disease involves excess fluid (endolymph) in the inner ear.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Endolymphatic hydrops — fluid buildup — is the underlying pathology.

scaffolds.q: Ménière's disease causes EPISODIC attacks of vertigo lasting minutes to hours.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Attacks come and go, often with warning signs like ear fullness.

scaffolds.q: Over time, Ménière's disease causes progressive hearing loss, especially low frequencies.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Repeated attacks damage hair cells, causing permanent hearing loss.

scaffolds.q: Conjunctivitis is an eye condition, strabismus is eye misalignment — neither causes hearing symptoms.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! These are eye disorders, not inner ear disorders.

scaffolds.q: The triad of tinnitus + vertigo + progressive hearing loss defines Ménière's syndrome.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Ménière's disease (endolymphatic hydrops) causes this classic symptom triad.
